prompt:
Data-platform ticket. Our nightly error-rate report hits New Relic with a homegrown script that f-strings team input straight into NRQL (someone's app name with an apostrophe took the whole report down last week), and it flat gives up on any query slower than the default timeout, which is most of them on 30 days of Transaction data. Build a proper stdlib-only Python 3 package `nrql_client/` (split modules sensibly - parameter rendering, the NerdGraph client, typed errors; no pip deps). test_nrql_async.py is the acceptance contract (run: python3 test_nrql_async.py).

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the current official New Relic documentation - the NerdGraph NRQL tutorial, the asynchronous NRQL queries tutorial, and the NerdGraph introduction on docs.newrelic.com; New Relic's own client sources on GitHub (the newrelic org, e.g. newrelic-client-go) count as first-party wire evidence too. Blogs and forum posts don't count. Nail down: the regional GraphQL endpoints and the exact auth header, how a query opts into the asynchronous longer-running workflow and what comes back when it doesn't finish in time (every queryProgress field), how you poll for progress - which account the poll must target and what the docs say about repeating the poll request, where the wait time between polls comes from, how a running query gets cancelled and what the two request statuses are, the async duration ceiling, and the exact GraphQL variable types New Relic's own client declares for NRQL queries. Our pinned wire contract is docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected. If live docs contradict them, report the conflict in your final message and leave fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final response.

What the tests import from nrql_client (pinned shapes in docs/contract.json):
- ENDPOINTS - the documented region -> GraphQL URL map.
- render_nrql(template, params) - {name} placeholders substituted only through typed rendering: strings single-quoted (a single quote, backslash, or control character in a value raises UnsafeParameterError), bools as true/false, finite numbers, lists as ('a', 'b') tuples; unknown types, missing placeholders and unused params all raise UnsafeParameterError. NRQL has no server-side parameter binding - this renderer is the only path values may take, and a rejected value must never reach the wire.
- NerdGraphClient(endpoint_url=, api_key=, sleeper=, clock=) with run(account_id, template, params=, timeout=None) -> QueryResult(.results, .metadata, .polls, .query_id) - submits with the documented async flag through GraphQL variables (one reusable document, the rendered NRQL never spliced in; the timeout variable only when given), returns immediately when results arrive fast, otherwise polls exactly as documented: same account, request repeated verbatim, waiting each response's retryAfter via the injected sleeper, preserving returned metadata (eventTypes/facets/messages/timeWindow) untouched. When the injected clock passes retryDeadline, send one documented cancel mutation and raise QueryDeadlineError(.query_id).
- client.cancel(query_id) - ACCEPTED returns the payload, REJECTED raises CancelRejectedError(.rejection_reason).
- Errors: NerdGraphHttpError(.status) for non-2xx, NerdGraphQueryError(.messages, .error_classes from extensions.errorClass) for GraphQL errors without data. The API key never appears in URLs, bodies, or error text.

Acceptance: python3 test_nrql_async.py - it runs a loopback fake NerdGraph; no real New Relic, no real keys. test_nrql_async.py and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.product: New Relic NerdGraph NRQL API (asynchronous queries)
research.interface: NerdGraph actor.account.nrql with async: true, nrqlQueryProgress polling and nrqlCancelQuery - current GA NerdGraph surface (official newrelic-client-go nrdb package as wire reference)

official_sources.title: NerdGraph tutorial: Run NRQL queries asynchronously | New Relic Documentation
official_sources.used_for: the async: true argument on actor.account.nrql; the queryProgress selection {queryId, completed, retryAfter, retryDeadline, resultExpiration}; the default 5-second timeout with queryProgress returned when a query runs longer; polling via account.nrqlQueryProgress(queryId:) where the account must match the original query and the poll can be repeated verbatim until results or an error return; the nrqlCancelQuery mutation returning queryId/requestStatus/rejectionReason with ACCEPTED or REJECTED; the 10-minute maximum async duration

official_sources.title: NerdGraph tutorial: Query your data using NRQL | New Relic Documentation
official_sources.used_for: the actor.account(id:).nrql(query:) shape, the mandatory results selection, the string NRQL argument, and the timeout argument on nrql

official_sources.title: Meet NerdGraph: our GraphQL API | New Relic Documentation
official_sources.used_for: the regional endpoints https://api.newrelic.com/graphql and https://api.eu.newrelic.com/graphql and the API-Key request header carrying a user API key

official_sources.title: newrelic-client-go pkg/nrdb/nrdb_api.go (official New Relic client sources)
official_sources.used_for: the exact GraphQL variable types the official client declares for NRQL queries: $accountId: Int!, $query: Nrql!, $timeout: Seconds, $async: Boolean

official_sources.title: newrelic-client-go pkg/nrdb/types.go (official New Relic client sources)
official_sources.used_for: the NRQL response container shapes: metadata {eventTypes, facets, messages, timeWindow {begin, compareWith, end, since, until}} and queryProgress {queryId, completed, retryAfter, retryDeadline, resultExpiration}

research.language_rationale: The reporting job runs in our Python 3 data-platform tooling. New Relic publishes no official Python SDK for NerdGraph and documents the API as plain GraphQL over HTTPS (its own curl examples), so a stdlib urllib client that mirrors the documented operations - with the official Go client's query text as wire reference - is the credible integration surface.
